payload.answer:
US Steel has actively pursued its planned merger with Nippon Steel since the announcement on December 18, 2023. The definitive agreement provides for a purchase price of $55.00 per share in cash, implying an equity value of approximately $14.1 billion and an enterprise value of about $14.9 billion (including assumed debt), representing a ~40% premium to US Steel’s closing price on December 15, 2023 [source 1; source 3]. The US Steel Board unanimously recommended the transaction, and shareholders approved it on April 12, 2024, with more than 98% of votes in favor [source 2; source 4].

To address business operations, US Steel stated that it would continue to operate under the "U.S. Steel" brand, maintain its Pittsburgh headquarters, and honor existing collective bargaining agreements [source 1]. Nippon Steel committed to no layoffs or plant closures until September 2026, to invest at least $1.4 billion after closing (including $1 billion for Mon Valley Works), and to retain union contracts [source 7]. The strategic rationale includes combining advanced technologies, expanding U.S. production, and increasing global crude steel capacity to 86 million tons per year [source 6].

However, President Biden blocked the transaction in January 2025. In response, US Steel and Nippon Steel filed lawsuits challenging the decision [source 8]. Consequently, the effect on business operations remains uncertain due to the ongoing legal challenge; prior operational commitments have not been implemented pending resolution.
